English
Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections
Diego Cortez No Wave Collection
Cortez, Diego
8120
1.5 cubic feet. 1.5 cubic feet.
1972-1981 Extent is approximate. Photographs, artwork, correspondence, manuscripts, and printed material. Approximately 135 pieces complied by Diego Cortez comprising of material related to both his 1981 No Wave show at PS1 and many of the personalities involved in the artistic and musical scenes of the period, including Jimmy DeSana, James Chance, Anya Phillips, Fab 5 Freddy, Bob Gruen, Robert Gordon, Glenn O'Brien, Legs O'Neil, Ray Johnson, Debbie Harry, and Jean-Michel Basquiat, among others. The collection also contains concert flyers, photographs, and exhibition materials, including early proposals for a book and exhibition on graffiti art. Collection material in English 9861354
General
Contact Information:
Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524rareref@cornell.eduhttp://rmc.library.cornell.edu
Compiled by:
RMC Staff
Date completed:
October 2018
EAD encoding:
Marcie Farwell, October 2018
Date modified:
RMC Staff, October 2018
BIOGRAPHICAL NOTE
Born James Allen Curtis in Geneva, Illinois in 1946, Curtis moved to New York City in 1973 and assumed the name Diego Cortez. He became well-known as an artist, club manager, promoter, and independent curator, co-founding the influential NYC nightspot The Mudd Club, which offered an eclectic mix of art, music and cultural events that combined both modernist and punk aesthetics. In 1981, Cortez curated the groundbreaking exhibition "New York, No Wave" at a defunct elementary school in Long Island City, Queens called P.S. 1. The exhibition featured the work of David Byrne, Keith Haring, Robert Mapplethorpe, Jean-Michel Basquiat, and Kenny Scharf, among many others.
COLLECTION DESCRIPTION
Approximately 135 pieces complied by Diego Cortez comprising of material related to both his 1981 No Wave show at PS1 and many of the personalities involved in the artistic and musical scenes of the period, including Jimmy DeSana, James Chance, Anya Phillips, Fab 5 Freddy, Bob Gruen, Robert Gordon, Glenn O'Brien, Legs O'Neil, Ray Johnson, Debbie Harry, and Jean-Michel Basquiat, among others. The collection also contains concert flyers, photographs, and exhibition materials, including early proposals for a book and exhibition on graffiti art.
SERIES LIST
Series I. Correspondence
Series II. Exhibitions
Series III. Manuscripts
Series IV. Periodicals/press materials
Series V. Photographs
Series VI. Posters and flyers
Series VII. Recordings and artwork
Cite As:
Diego Cortez No Wave Collection, #8120. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.
New York (N.Y.). Punk rock music. Punk culture. No wave (Music). Nightlife. Nightclubs. Graffiti artists. Graffiti. Avant-garde (Music). Avant-garde (Aesthetics) -- New York (State) -- New York -- Exhibitions. Avant-garde (Aesthetics) -- New York (State) -- New York. Avant garde. De Sana, Jimmy.
9861354 <<<<<<< HEAD CorrespondenceLetter by Marina Abramovic to Diego CortezAbramovic , MarinaTyped letter with signature and flower drawing in ink, written from Belgrade, Yugoslavia. Marina invites Cortez to participate in 'April Meeting' performance festival in Belgrade.11Handwritten letter from Jimmy DeSana to Diego Cortez1975Red ink on paper, signed by DeSana with artist's stamp of camera/hands at top. With envelope with 'CORTEZ' handwritten on it.11Postcard to Diego and Jim DeSana from "cf"11Letter by Liz Rhodes to Diego CortezTwo-sided handwritten letter with signature, written from London, with notations by Cortez. At the behest of Peter Gidal, Rhodes invites Cortez to show films at the London Film Cooperative.11Letter to DC from Udo BregerOne page letter with attached photograph of Breger.11
Scope and Contents
DC met Breger through William S. Burroughs. Breger was the editor of the Swiss Journal SOFT NEED.Bonn, GermanyLetter to DC c/o Betsy Sussler from Udo BregerTwo small pages, one with attached photo of Breger. In postmarked envelope.11Gotteborg, GermanyLetter by Peter Gidal to "Hernando Cortez"Signed.11
Scope and Contents
Experimental filmmaker Peter Gidal also head of London Filmmakers Cooperative. Aerogramme letter by Gidal from London re: possible film screenings by DC in London.Letter by Norman Rosenthal to Diego CortezTwo-sided handwritten letter by Rosenthal from London. Signature on verso.11
Scope and Contents
He sums up the state of affairs at the I.C.A. and other venues in London.Letter from Pasquale Trisorio to Diego11Postcard from Marek Konieczny, WarsawSigned by Makarewicz.11
Scope and Contents
Re: film screening at Galeria WspolszesnaLetter by Zbigniew Makarewicz to Consulate General of Poland to the US11
Scope and Contents
Requesting artistic visa for Cortez re: a film screening in Warsaw.Letter by Makarewicz to Cortez11
Scope and Contents
Re: film screening at Galeria Wspolszensna, WarsawTyped letter by Diego Cortez to Candida HÃ¶ffer11
Scope and Contents
Re: Cortez's upcoming 'SMEAR' exhibition at Konrad Fischer Gallery, Dusseldorf.Letter by Diego Cortez to Robin WintersTwo-sided typed letter with handwritten notations in ink, written from Germany.11
Scope and Contents
Cortez brings Winters up to date on several European projects.Postcard from Ingrid Oppenheim to DCOppenheim, Ingrid11
Scope and Contents
"Greetings from Ingrid Oppenheim [Koln gallerist]." DC met Oppenheim through Dennis Oppenheim, for whom DC first worked for in NYC in 1973-74.Cologne, GermanyLetter from DeSana to DCDeSana, JimmyHandwritten. In handwritten envelope "Por Diego Cortez."11
Scope and Contents
DeSana brings DC up to date on all recent affairs, including the fact that DC ex-roommate Jane Kaplowitz will now move in with historian Robert Rosenblum. DeSana and DC always referred to each other as 'Doctor' and here refers to Jane as 'Nurse.' Also gossip about art critics David Bourdon and Gregory Battcock.Postcard from UK to Diego CortezPhillips, Anya11New York, New YorkMailgram11
Scope and Contents
From Michael Zilkha and Michel Esteban to Cortez, Ze Records.Fab Five FreddyOffset on card stock.11
Scope and Contents
Calling card.Draft of a letter to Yossy AorenUndated11
Scope and Contents
Regarding possible show in Israel.ExhibitionsXerox and BloodMoMA security guard notes for 1975 'Xerox and Blood' exhibitionCortez, DiegoPhotograph contact sheet, notebook pages, handwritten in ink, typed letter from Head of MoMA security on MoMA stationary, bank deposit slip, MoMA check stub, note card, etc.
Scope and Contents
Cortez was employed as a security guard at MoMA in 1974-75. He was invited to make a show at the Whitney Museum Art Resources Center. The exhibition was titled 'Xerox and Blood' and opened in Spring, 1975. Cortez's made several conceptual wall series which dealt with the issues of security and art. One series reflects on the attack on Picasso's Guernica by artist Tony Shafrazi.Manila envelopeHand-titled by Cortez.13Images taken at MoMA security officeOne photo contact sheet, including arrest pictures of Tony Shafrazi.13Gelatin silver prints.MoMA security work ephemera30 assorted handwritten-in-ink notebook pages from MoMA security work for Whitney Downtown Museum exhibition by Cortez (most written on both sides); one check stub, one deposit slip, one letter on MoMA letterhead from Head of Security Dept.13Works from 1975 Whitney Downtown Museum showCortez, Diego75 Xerox works.14Announcements for 1975 "Xerox and Blood" show at Whitney Downtown MuseumCortez, Diego110 Xerox announcements.15New York, New YorkInstallation plan for 1975 Whitney Downtown Museum showCortez, Diego13
Scope and Contents
Installation plan.Cataratta Riflesso RossoNotes for photo series 'Cataratta Riflesso Rosso' for 1976 Galleria Trisorio exhibitionBlue ink on paper.16Napoli, ItalySketches for wall paintings for 1976 'Cataratta Riflesso Rosso' exhibition, Galleria TrisorioCortez, DiegoRed and blue inks on found letterhead. Nine pages.16Napoli, ItalySketches, notes re: medical photo works in "Cataratta Riflesso Rosso"16
Scope and Contents
Exhibition at Studio Pasquale TrisorioNapoli, ItalyPrints documenting Cortez's gallery exhibition 'Cataratta Riflesso Rosso' at Galleria Trisoriodel Monaco, BrunoContact prints. Both stamped by del Monaco on verso and numbered by pencil.16Napoli, ItalyGelatin silver prints.Smear, The Batman Show, and Hospital ColorsKodachrome slidesCortez, Diego12
Scope and Contents
3 original slides used in Cortez's lecture/performance 'Hospital Colors' performed at 'April Meeting' in Belgrade (1975) and Artists Space, NYC (1976). The lecture analyzed the chromatics of institutional interiors.Study for announcement card for Diego Cortez "SMEAR" exhibitionCortez, DiegoColor Xerox.18
Scope and Contents
Galerie Konrad Fischer.Dusseldorf, GermanyTwo-sided handwritten note by Diego Cortez16
Scope and Contents
Note is earliest formulation of the future 1981 'New York/New Wave' show at PS1. Ink and typed text on paper card.Notes for "The Batman Show"Cortez, Diego17
Scope and Contents
This was the first exhibition which Cortez curated as well as the first exhibition sponsored by Collaborative Projects (COLAB). The exhibition took place at Robin Winters loft in Soho. These are the only notes documenting the organization of that show. Xerox. Signed.Batman's Left NippleJohnson, RayCollage, mirror mounted on red construction paper.17
Scope and Contents
Johnson's submission to Cortez's 1977 exhibition 'The Batman Show', sponsored by Collaborative Projects (Colab), held at Robin Winters' Soho loft in 1977. Hand printed title and artist's stamp on face of work.New York/New WaveInitial List of Artists, "New York/New Wave" exhibition (1981)Cortez, DiegoTyped list of artists, 6 pages.19Rene RicardArmstrong, DavidSigned and titled with notations on verso (handwritten specs as to larger print to be made for 'New York/New Wave show, dated '1980').19
Scope and Contents
From the New York/New Wave exhibitionNew York, New YorkGelatin silver prints.Untitled (New York/New Wave)Maripol128
Scope and Contents
2 unique hand-colored Polaroids. These two photographs were exhibited in Cortez's 1981 'New York/New Wave' exhibition at P.S.1.diffusion transfer printsGraffitiRemarks on Graffiti (Unpublished)Smith, DuncanPhotocopy with hand-written edits and notations by Cortez. 13 pages.110Graffiti 1980, America's Underground Art Come of Age, Subterranean Calligraphy, 1981 | From Steel to Canvas, The Graffiti 1980 Experience, Zoo York Magazine, 1980Van Daal, VincentTwo typewritten manuscripts on Graffiti. 10 pages.110Graffiti Exhibition ProposalCortez, DiegoBlue envelope with hand-written title by Cortez.110Berlin, GermanyRome, ItalyOriginal Proposal Notes for Unrealized Graffiti Exhibition Project, SpringCortez, DiegoSix typed pages.110Berlin, GermanyRome, ItalyScreeningsNotes for screenings in European cinematheque tour by CortezCortez, Diego7-paged handwritten notes.111
Scope and Contents
At the behest of Jonas Mekas and P. Adams Sitney, Cortez made a tour of European cinematheques and showed a series of films made for the analytic projector which converts a film screening into a 'film performance'. Red ink and black marker on found letterhead with visual diagrams of stills from filmsNotes for screenings in European cinematheque tour by CortezCortez, Diego111
Scope and Contents
At the behest of Jonas Mekas and P. Adams Sitney, Cortez made a tour of European cinematheques and showed a series of films made for the analytic projector which converts a film screening into a 'film performance'. Red ink and black marker on found letterhead with visual diagrams of stills from films.Notes on MusÃ©e du CinÃ©maCortez, DiegoOne-page typed notes on Brussels letterhead111
Scope and Contents
re: DC 1975-76 European and US tour of exhibitions, performances and film screenings (not all realized).Bio and Notes on Screening for Candida Hoffer2-paged xerox.111
Scope and Contents
Re: Cortez's upcoming 'SMEAR' exhibition at Konrad Fischer Gallery, Dusseldorf.Notes for "Poisoning"Cortez, Diego111
Scope and Contents
Including cues. Analytic projector film to be screened in US and Europe. Two pages, Xerox with handwritten notes.Notes re: possible programming of films by Diego CortezCortez, Diego5 pgs. typewritten111
Scope and Contents
Notes on analytic projector specifications and DC biography. Stadtmuseum.Munchen, GermanyNotes re: "New American Performance" proposal for Stadtische KunsthalleCortez, Diego111Dusseldorf, GermanyManuscriptsAPO-33 Bulletin, A Metabolic RegulatorBurroughs, William S.Signed by DC on page 1.112San Francisco, CaliforniaBeach Books, Distributed by City LightsNotes for "Cansearch Report"Cortez, Diego112
Scope and Contents
These notes were made for an unrealized artist book commissioned by Mario Diacono, Bologna. 4 typewritten pages with handwritten notes in ink and pencil.Storyboads for William S. Burroughs film project, Blade RunnerCortez, Diego4
Scope and Contents
Extracts from Burroughs' text with notations and collage elements by Cortez, mounted on drawing paper pad. In 1979, Cortez asked Burroughs and his assistant, James Grauerholz, for the film rights for the book and was granted permission. The film was never realized. Burroughs' book is a sci-fi novella which began as a story treatment for a proposed film adaptation of Alan E. Nourse's novel 'The Bladerunner.' Burroughs' treatment has been described as a closet screenplay. It is set in the early 21st century and involves mutated viruses and a medical-care apocalypse. The term 'blade runner' refers to a smuggler of medical supplies, e.g. scalpels. No film was ever made from Burroughs' text. The title was later bought by Ridley Scott for use in his 1982 science fiction film, 'Blade Runner.' Its plot was based on Philip K. Dick's 'Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?' and not the Nourse/ Burroughs material, although the dialogue incorporates the term 'blade runner' into dialogue.Periodicals and press materialsArt Rite, Issue No. Ten, Fall113
Scope and Contents
With participation of Diego Cortez ('Notes on Painter/Patient Performance') and other artists. Bronson, and Lucy LippardFile Magazine, "New York City" Edition, Spring21

Unknown photographer (possibly David Godlis).Gelatin silver prints.Diego Cortez (Esoterrorists)Phillips, Anya1978117
Scope and Contents
Gift of artist to Diego Cortez. The 'Esoterrorists' were an imaginary NYC punk band consisting of Anya Phillips, Duncan Smith and Diego Cortez). They only existed as documented by various photos and published in art and music magazines and zines in 1978-79.Gelatin silver prints.EsoterroristsVogl, Harald1978Unsigned.117
Scope and Contents
Gift of artist to Diego Cortez. This incarnation of the 'Esoterrorists' was as an imaginary Munich punk band consisting of Anya Phillips, Charlie Heil, Harald Vogl and Diego Cortez. These photos by Vogl remain unpublished.Munich, GermanyGelatin silver prints.Andy Warhol, Diego Cortez and othersGrossman, Bobby1978117Gelatin silver prints.Andy Warhol, Diego CortezGrossman, Bobby1978117Gelatin silver prints.Anya Phillips, Tim Guest, Diego CortezGrossman, Bobby1978117Gelatin silver prints.Brian EnoDeSana, Jimmy1978117Gelatin silver prints.Diego CortezDeSana, Jimmy197822New York, New YorkGelatin silver prints.Boris Policeband, Diego CortezStarr, Michael1979Signed and titled on verso.118

Gift of artist to Diego Cortez. Taken by Gruen at the Club Fez, in the East Village at a poetry reading in which all the subjects pictured read.Gelatin silver prints.Bockris, VictorRoss, DavidLindsay, ArtoUntitled (Modena, Italy)Basquiat, Jean-Michel1981Unsigned and mounted in window matte.23
Scope and Contents
Six vintage Polaroids. Gift of artist to Diego Cortez. Cortez organized Basquiat's first solo gallery exhibition at Galleria Emilio Mazzoli, which opened May, 1981. Cortez accompanied Basquiat to Modena and installed the show. These were part of about 20 Polaroids Basquiat took in Modena's Piazza Grande during the installation period.Modena, Italydiffusion transfer printsPhoto booth portraitUndated118UntitledUndated118

DC worked for Acconci and set up his installation; this is when DC met Boetti and was invited to stay at his Rome home the following summer,1975, where Boetti introduced DC to his protege, Francesco ClementeMarden, BriceAcconci, VitoBoetti, AlighieriRockburne, DorotheaBuren, DanielDC performance/film screening at Anthology Film ArchivesCortez, Diego1975-04Offset on card stock.124New York, New YorkPerformance/video event at Kvindeudstillingen, CharlottenborgAbramovic, Marina1975-12Printers ink on card stock.124

Exhibited: "Xerox and Blood", Whitney Museum Art Resources Center, Spring 1975; "40-The Anniversary Exhibition", Hal Bromm Gallery, NYC, 2015-16.New York, New YorkSeven collage worksCortez, Diego1976Mixed media mounted on butcher paper.24

Includes "Sway Back" and "Palming".Artwork by othersEl grupo de los trece en arte de sistemasBeuys, Joseph1972-1973127CAYC Arte ArgentinoUntitled (Prinzendorf 24-hour Aktion)Nitsch, Hermann1975Animal blood on linen.25
Scope and Contents
DC was a principal actor in Nitsch's first 24-hour Aktion. Gift from Nitsch to DC following 1975 Aktion. ======= CorrespondenceLetter by Marina Abramovic to Diego CortezAbramovic , MarinaTyped letter with signature and flower drawing in ink, written from Belgrade, Yugoslavia. Marina invites Cortez to participate in 'April Meeting' performance festival in Belgrade.11Handwritten letter from Jimmy DeSana to Diego Cortez1975Red ink on paper, signed by DeSana with artist's stamp of camera/hands at top. With envelope with 'CORTEZ' handwritten on it.11Postcard to Diego and Jim DeSana from "cf"11Letter by Liz Rhodes to Diego CortezTwo-sided handwritten letter with signature, written from London, with notations by Cortez. At the behest of Peter Gidal, Rhodes invites Cortez to show films at the London Film Cooperative.11Letter to DC from Udo BregerOne page letter with attached photograph of Breger.11
Scope and Contents
DC met Breger through William S. Burroughs. Breger was the editor of the Swiss Journal SOFT NEED.Bonn, GermanyLetter to DC c/o Betsy Sussler from Udo BregerTwo small pages, one with attached photo of Breger. In postmarked envelope.11Gotteborg, GermanyLetter by Peter Gidal to "Hernando Cortez"Signed.11
Scope and Contents
Experimental filmmaker Peter Gidal also head of London Filmmakers Cooperative. Aerogramme letter by Gidal from London re: possible film screenings by DC in London.Letter by Norman Rosenthal to Diego CortezTwo-sided handwritten letter by Rosenthal from London. Signature on verso.11
Scope and Contents
He sums up the state of affairs at the I.C.A. and other venues in London.Letter from Pasquale Trisorio to Diego11Postcard from Marek Konieczny, WarsawSigned by Makarewicz.11

Re: Cortez's upcoming 'SMEAR' exhibition at Konrad Fischer Gallery, Dusseldorf.Letter by Diego Cortez to Robin WintersTwo-sided typed letter with handwritten notations in ink, written from Germany.11
Scope and Contents
Cortez brings Winters up to date on several European projects.Postcard from Ingrid Oppenheim to DCOppenheim, Ingrid11
Scope and Contents
"Greetings from Ingrid Oppenheim [Koln gallerist]." DC met Oppenheim through Dennis Oppenheim, for whom DC first worked for in NYC in 1973-74.Cologne, GermanyLetter from DeSana to DCDeSana, JimmyHandwritten. In handwritten envelope "Por Diego Cortez."11
Scope and Contents
DeSana brings DC up to date on all recent affairs, including the fact that DC ex-roommate Jane Kaplowitz will now move in with historian Robert Rosenblum. DeSana and DC always referred to each other as 'Doctor' and here refers to Jane as 'Nurse.' Also gossip about art critics David Bourdon and Gregory Battcock.Postcard from UK to Diego CortezPhillips, Anya11New York, New YorkMailgram11
Scope and Contents
From Michael Zilkha and Michel Esteban to Cortez, Ze Records.Fab Five FreddyOffset on card stock.11
Scope and Contents
Calling card.Draft of a letter to Yossy AorenUndated11
Scope and Contents
Regarding possible show in Israel.ExhibitionsXerox and BloodMoMA security guard notes for 1975 'Xerox and Blood' exhibitionCortez, DiegoPhotograph contact sheet, notebook pages, handwritten in ink, typed letter from Head of MoMA security on MoMA stationary, bank deposit slip, MoMA check stub, note card, etc.
Scope and Contents
Cortez was employed as a security guard at MoMA in 1974-75. He was invited to make a show at the Whitney Museum Art Resources Center. The exhibition was titled 'Xerox and Blood' and opened in Spring, 1975. Cortez's made several conceptual wall series which dealt with the issues of security and art. One series reflects on the attack on Picasso's Guernica by artist Tony Shafrazi.Manila envelopeHand-titled by Cortez.13Images taken at MoMA security officeOne photo contact sheet, including arrest pictures of Tony Shafrazi.13Gelatin silver prints.MoMA security work ephemera30 assorted handwritten-in-ink notebook pages from MoMA security work for Whitney Downtown Museum exhibition by Cortez (most written on both sides); one check stub, one deposit slip, one letter on MoMA letterhead from Head of Security Dept.13Works from 1975 Whitney Downtown Museum showCortez, Diego75 Xerox works.14Announcements for 1975 "Xerox and Blood" show at Whitney Downtown MuseumCortez, Diego110 Xerox announcements.15New York, New YorkInstallation plan for 1975 Whitney Downtown Museum showCortez, Diego13
Scope and Contents
Installation plan.Cataratta Riflesso RossoNotes for photo series 'Cataratta Riflesso Rosso' for 1976 Galleria Trisorio exhibitionBlue ink on paper.16Napoli, ItalySketches for wall paintings for 1976 'Cataratta Riflesso Rosso' exhibition, Galleria TrisorioCortez, DiegoRed and blue inks on found letterhead. Nine pages.16Napoli, ItalySketches, notes re: medical photo works in "Cataratta Riflesso Rosso"16

Both stamped by del Monaco on verso and numbered by pencil.16Napoli, ItalyGelatin silver prints.Smear, The Batman Show, and Hospital ColorsKodachrome slidesCortez, Diego12
Scope and Contents
3 original slides used in Cortez's lecture/performance 'Hospital Colors' performed at 'April Meeting' in Belgrade (1975) and Artists Space, NYC (1976). The lecture analyzed the chromatics of institutional interiors.Study for announcement card for Diego Cortez "SMEAR" exhibitionCortez, DiegoColor Xerox.18

Galerie Konrad Fischer.Dusseldorf, GermanyTwo-sided handwritten note by Diego Cortez16
Scope and Contents
Note is earliest formulation of the future 1981 'New York/New Wave' show at PS1. Ink and typed text on paper card.Notes for "The Batman Show"Cortez, Diego17
Scope and Contents
This was the first exhibition which Cortez curated as well as the first exhibition sponsored by Collaborative Projects (COLAB). The exhibition took place at Robin Winters loft in Soho. These are the only notes documenting the organization of that show. Xerox. Signed.Batman's Left NippleJohnson, RayCollage, mirror mounted on red construction paper.17
Scope and Contents
Johnson's submission to Cortez's 1977 exhibition 'The Batman Show', sponsored by Collaborative Projects (Colab), held at Robin Winters' Soho loft in 1977. Scope and Contents
At the behest of Jonas Mekas and P. Adams Sitney, Cortez made a tour of European cinematheques and showed a series of films made for the analytic projector which converts a film screening into a 'film performance'. Red ink and black marker on found letterhead with visual diagrams of stills from filmsNotes for screenings in European cinematheque tour by CortezCortez, Diego111

Stadtmuseum.Munchen, GermanyNotes re: "New American Performance" proposal for Stadtische KunsthalleCortez, Diego111Dusseldorf, GermanyManuscriptsAPO-33 Bulletin, A Metabolic RegulatorBurroughs, William S.Signed by DC on page 1.112San Francisco, CaliforniaBeach Books, Distributed by City LightsNotes for "Cansearch Report"Cortez, Diego112
Scope and Contents
These notes were made for an unrealized artist book commissioned by Mario Diacono, Bologna. 4 typewritten pages with handwritten notes in ink and pencil.Storyboads for William S. Burroughs film project, Blade RunnerCortez, Diego4
Scope and Contents
Extracts from Burroughs' text with notations and collage elements by Cortez, mounted on drawing paper pad. In 1979, Cortez asked Burroughs and his assistant, James Grauerholz, for the film rights for the book and was granted permission. The film was never realized. Burroughs' book is a sci-fi novella which began as a story treatment for a proposed film adaptation of Alan E. Nourse's novel 'The Bladerunner.' Burroughs' treatment has been described as a closet screenplay. It is set in the early 21st century and involves mutated viruses and a medical-care apocalypse. The term 'blade runner' refers to a smuggler of medical supplies, e.g. scalpels. No film was ever made from Burroughs' text. The title was later bought by Ridley Scott for use in his 1982 science fiction film, 'Blade Runner.' Its plot was based on Philip K. Dick's 'Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?' and not the Nourse/ Burroughs material, although the dialogue incorporates the term 'blade runner' into dialogue.Periodicals and press materialsArt Rite, Issue No. Ten, Fall113
Scope and Contents
With participation of Diego Cortez ('Notes on Painter/Patient Performance') and other artists. Bronson, and Lucy LippardFile Magazine, "New York City" Edition, Spring21

Scope and Contents
Gift of artist to Diego Cortez. The 'Esoterrorists' were an imaginary NYC punk band consisting of Anya Phillips, Duncan Smith and Diego Cortez). They only existed as documented by various photos and published in art and music magazines and zines in 1978-79.Gelatin silver prints.EsoterroristsVogl, Harald1978Unsigned.117
Scope and Contents
Gift of artist to Diego Cortez. This incarnation of the 'Esoterrorists' was as an imaginary Munich punk band consisting of Anya Phillips, Charlie Heil, Harald Vogl and Diego Cortez. Scope and Contents
Six vintage Polaroids. Gift of artist to Diego Cortez. Cortez organized Basquiat's first solo gallery exhibition at Galleria Emilio Mazzoli, which opened May, 1981. Cortez accompanied Basquiat to Modena and installed the show. These were part of about 20 Polaroids Basquiat took in Modena's Piazza Grande during the installation period.Modena, Italydiffusion transfer printsPhoto booth portraitUndated118UntitledUndated118
